content.text: Availability, scalability, and elasticity are three of the most frequently cited cloud benefits and appear explicitly in the AZ-900 exam objectives. They are related but distinct: availability answers whether a service is reachable; scalability answers whether it can handle increased workload; elasticity answers whether it can adjust capacity automatically in response to changing demand.

On-premises infrastructure struggles with all three — high availability requires expensive redundant hardware, scalability requires purchasing headroom months in advance, and elasticity is essentially impossible without automation that most on-premises environments lack. The cloud addresses all three through shared infrastructure, pay-as-you-go billing, and managed platform services.

attrs.alt: Three-column diagram: Availability (blue, SLA tiers and AZs), Scalability (green, vertical vs horizontal), Elasticity (orange, demand curve with auto-scaling)

content.text: Availability is the percentage of time a service is up and accessible. It is expressed in the form of a Service Level Agreement (SLA) — a contract between Microsoft and the customer specifying the guaranteed uptime percentage. If Azure fails to meet the SLA in a calendar month, customers receive service credits.
The difference between SLA tiers is not linear — a small percentage improvement represents a dramatic reduction in allowed downtime:
VMs in Availability Set; AKS Standard tier
VMs across Availability Zones; Azure SQL Business Critical
Azure Front Door global routing + redundant origins

The primary mechanism for achieving high availability in Azure is deploying across Availability Zones. Each zone is a physically separate datacenter within the same region with independent power, cooling, and networking:

# Deploy a zone-redundant Azure SQL Database (Business Critical, 99.995% SLA)
az sql server create \
  --name myapp-sql \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password 'P@ssw0rd1234!'

az sql db create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --server myapp-sql \
  --name myapp-db \
  --service-objective BC_Gen5_4 \
  --zone-redundant true
  # BC_Gen5_4 = Business Critical, Gen5, 4 vCores; zone-redundant → 99.995% SLA

# Verify zone redundancy status
az sql db show \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --server myapp-sql \
  --name myapp-db \
  --query '{tier:currentServiceObjectiveName, zoneRedundant:zoneRedundant}'
When an application depends on multiple Azure services, the composite SLA is the product of each individual SLA. This has practical design implications:
Adding a third 99.9% dependency drops the composite to
— over 13 hours of allowed downtime per year
Exam tip: Azure does not guarantee 100% availability for any service. If an exam question asks for the composite SLA of a multi-service architecture, multiply the individual percentages together.

Scalability is the ability of a system to handle an increasing workload by adding resources. The key distinction from elasticity is that scalability is about designed capacity — it may involve manual operator action, automated rules, or architectural patterns. Scalability has two directions:
Vertical scaling adds more compute power to an existing resource — replacing a 2-vCPU VM with an 8-vCPU VM, or increasing a database tier. Azure supports vertical scaling of many services:

# Scale up a VM: resize from Standard_D2s_v5 to Standard_D8s_v5
az vm deallocate --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm
az vm resize \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-vm \
  --size Standard_D8s_v5
az vm start --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm

# Scale up App Service Plan (no downtime for P-tier resizes)
az appservice plan update \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-plan \
  --sku P3v3
  # P1v3 (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM) → P3v3 (8 vCPU, 32 GB RAM)

# Scale up Cosmos DB: increase provisioned throughput
az cosmosdb sql container throughput update \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --account-name myapp-cosmos \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --throughput 10000  # increase from 400 RU/s to 10,000 RU/s
Vertical scaling has a ceiling: Azure VM SKUs go up to 192 vCPUs and 4 TB of RAM. Beyond these limits — or when per-unit cost becomes prohibitive — horizontal scaling is required.

Horizontal scaling adds more instances of the same size and distributes work via a load balancer. It has no theoretical upper bound. The prerequisite is stateless application design — session state must live externally (Azure Cache for Redis, Cosmos DB) so any instance can serve any request:

# Create a VM Scale Set with 3 initial instances, max 50
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-vmss \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --instance-count 3 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --load-balancer myapp-lb \
  --upgrade-policy-mode Rolling

# Manually scale out to 10 instances (scalability — deliberate, not automatic)
az vmss scale \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-vmss \
  --new-capacity 10

# Verify
az vmss list-instances \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-vmss \
  --query '[].{ID:instanceId,State:provisioningState}' -o table

Elasticity is scalability with automation — the system adds capacity when demand rises and removes it when demand falls, without manual intervention. This automatic right-sizing is what makes cloud economics work: instead of provisioning for peak load at all times, you provision for the current load and the platform adjusts.
Azure Monitor Autoscale provides metric-based and schedule-based scaling for VMs, VMSS, App Service, and AKS:

# Create autoscale settings for an App Service Plan
az monitor autoscale create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --resource myapp-plan \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Web/serverfarms \
  --name myapp-autoscale \
  --min-count 2 --max-count 20 --count 2

# Scale OUT: add 2 instances when avg CPU > 70% for 5 min
az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg --autoscale-name myapp-autoscale \
  --condition "Percentage CPU > 70 avg 5m" \
  --scale out 2

# Scale IN: remove 1 instance when avg CPU < 25% for 15 min
# Longer cooldown on scale-in prevents thrashing
az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg --autoscale-name myapp-autoscale \
  --condition "Percentage CPU < 25 avg 15m" \
  --scale in 1

# Scheduled profile: minimum 5 instances during business hours
az monitor autoscale profile create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg --autoscale-name myapp-autoscale \
  --name BusinessHours \
  --min-count 5 --max-count 20 --count 5 \
  --recurrence week \
  --recurrence-timezone "Eastern Standard Time" \
  --start 09:00 --end 18:00 \
  --weekdays Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday Friday

Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling (KEDA) extends AKS elasticity beyond CPU/memory. KEDA scales deployments based on queue depth, event hub consumer lag, or custom metrics — and supports scaling to zero:

# KEDA ScaledObject: scale based on Azure Service Bus queue depth
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: orders-processor-scaler
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: orders-processor        # Deployment to scale
  minReplicaCount: 0              # Scale to zero when queue is empty
  maxReplicaCount: 50
  pollingInterval: 30             # Check every 30 seconds
  cooldownPeriod: 300             # Wait 5 min before scaling in
  triggers:
    - type: azure-servicebus
      metadata:
        queueName: orders
        namespace: myapp-servicebus
        messageCount: "5"         # 1 replica per 5 queue messages
      authenticationRef:
        name: servicebus-trigger-auth

With minReplicaCount set to 0, the deployment scales to zero when the queue is empty — paying nothing for idle compute. When messages arrive, KEDA triggers scale-out from zero within seconds. This is the most cost-efficient elasticity pattern for batch and event-driven workloads.

Azure Functions on the Consumption plan represents the extreme of elasticity: instances are created on demand per invocation, scale from zero to hundreds concurrently, and you pay only per execution (billed in 100ms increments). There is no reserved capacity to manage:

import azure.functions as func
import logging

app = func.FunctionApp(http_auth_level=func.AuthLevel.FUNCTION)

@app.route(route="process-order")
def process_order(req: func.HttpRequest) -> func.HttpResponse:
    logging.info("Processing order")
    order_id = req.params.get("orderId")
    if not order_id:
        return func.HttpResponse("Missing orderId", status_code=400)

    # Stateless: each invocation is independent — safe on any instance
    result = f"order-{order_id}-complete"
    return func.HttpResponse(result, status_code=200)

# Billing on Consumption plan:
# - First 1,000,000 executions/month: FREE
# - Execution time: $0.000016 per GB-second
# - A function using 512 MB for 200ms = 0.0016 GB-s per invocation
# - 10 million such invocations: ~$160/month vs always-on VM ~$140/month minimum
VM resize, VMSS, App Service plan tier change
Autoscale, KEDA, Functions Consumption plan, Cosmos DB autoscale RU/s

Elasticity is a superset of scalability — an elastic system is scalable, but a scalable system is not necessarily elastic. A VM you manually resize each month is scalable; a VMSS that adds instances based on CPU rules is elastic.
"App must remain running if one datacenter loses power" →
"Website needs to handle 10× traffic on Black Friday" →
— autoscale adds instances when load spikes; removes them afterward.
"Organisation plans for long-term user growth with larger VMs" →
Availability Sets protect against rack-level failures and planned maintenance. They do
protect against zone-level failures — use Availability Zones for that.
Azure Functions (Consumption plan) is the canonical
example in AZ-900: it scales from zero automatically and charges per invocation.
Designing for High Availability: Patterns and Anti-Patterns

High availability is achieved through a combination of platform features and application-level design. The platform can protect against hardware failures, but it cannot protect against application bugs, database deadlocks, or deployment errors without the right architectural patterns.

Azure Load Balancer and Application Gateway use health probes to detect unhealthy instances and stop routing traffic to them. Applications must implement a health endpoint that accurately reflects their readiness:

# FastAPI: health endpoint that validates DB and cache connectivity
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response
import asyncpg
import redis.asyncio as redis
import os

app = FastAPI()
_db_pool = None
_redis = None

@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup():
    global _db_pool, _redis
    _db_pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(os.environ["DATABASE_URL"])
    _redis = redis.from_url(os.environ["REDIS_URL"])

@app.get("/health")
async def health(response: Response):
    checks = {}
    try:
        await _db_pool.fetchval("SELECT 1")
        checks["database"] = "ok"
    except Exception as e:
        checks["database"] = f"error: {e}"

    try:
        await _redis.ping()
        checks["cache"] = "ok"
    except Exception as e:
        checks["cache"] = f"error: {e}"

    healthy = all(v == "ok" for v in checks.values())
    response.status_code = 200 if healthy else 503
    return {"status": "healthy" if healthy else "degraded", "checks": checks}

# Azure Load Balancer probe: HTTP GET /health, port 8000, interval 15s, threshold 2
# Traffic stops routing to this instance within 30s of /health returning 503
Availability Set vs Availability Zone: When to Use Each
Fault domains (5 max) + update domains (20 max)
Regions without AZs; legacy IaaS architectures
All new production deployments where AZ is available

In regions that support Availability Zones, always prefer zone deployment over Availability Sets for new workloads. Availability Sets exist primarily for backward compatibility and for regions where AZs are not yet available.
Right-Sizing and the Economics of Elasticity

Elasticity's primary economic benefit is eliminating over-provisioning. Without autoscale, teams provision for the peak load they expect — leaving capacity idle the rest of the time. With elasticity, capacity tracks actual demand, reducing the average idle cost:

// Azure Monitor: identify over-provisioned VMs using 30-day CPU utilisation data
// Run in Log Analytics workspace
Perf
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ObjectName == "Processor" and CounterName == "% Processor Time"
| where InstanceName == "_Total"
| summarize
    AvgCPU = avg(CounterValue),
    P95CPU = percentile(CounterValue, 95),
    MaxCPU = max(CounterValue)
  by Computer
| where P95CPU < 20                  // P95 CPU below 20% = significantly over-provisioned
| project Computer, AvgCPU=round(AvgCPU,1), P95CPU=round(P95CPU,1), MaxCPU=round(MaxCPU,1)
| order by AvgCPU asc

// Azure Advisor also surfaces rightsizing recommendations automatically:
// az advisor recommendation list --category Cost --query "[?contains(shortDescription.solution, 'right-size')]" -o table

Autoscale rules include a cooldown period — a mandatory wait after a scale action before the next one fires. Without cooldown, a scale-out event followed immediately by reduced load could trigger a scale-in, then another scale-out in rapid succession ("flapping"). Best practices:
: Keep short (3–5 minutes). You want to respond quickly to rising demand.
: Keep longer (10–20 minutes). Removing instances too aggressively causes thrashing and can impact users mid-session.
: Set scale-in threshold significantly below scale-out threshold. If you scale out at CPU > 70%, scale in at CPU < 30% (not < 69%).
: Always set a minimum instance count ≥ 1 (or ≥ 2 for zone redundancy). Never configure autoscale to scale to zero for web-facing services that need immediate availability.
Observability and Monitoring for All Three Pillars
Each pillar requires specific monitoring to verify that Azure is meeting commitments and to alert when thresholds are approached:

# Monitor availability: alert when resource health changes to Unavailable
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name ResourceHealthAlert \
  --condition category=ResourceHealth status=Active \
  --action-group /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Insights/actionGroups/on-call

# Monitor scaling: alert when autoscale fires
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name AutoscaleFireAlert \
  --condition category=Autoscale \
  --action-group /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Insights/actionGroups/on-call

# Monitor performance: alert when P99 response time exceeds SLO
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name P99LatencyAlert \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --scopes /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/myapp \
  --condition "avg HttpResponseTime > 1.5" \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --severity 2
Cosmos DB: Native Availability, Scalability, and Elasticity
Azure Cosmos DB is the Azure service that most comprehensively embodies all three pillars simultaneously. It is worth understanding as a concrete end-to-end example:
: 99.999% SLA for multi-region, multi-write deployments. Data is replicated to all configured regions within milliseconds. Failover is automatic.
: Partition-key-based horizontal partitioning means there is no practical data size limit. Physical partitions split automatically as data and throughput grow.
: Autoscale throughput mode automatically adjusts Request Units per second (RU/s) between a configured minimum and maximum, charging only for what is consumed each hour.

# Cosmos DB: configure autoscale throughput (elastic RU/s)
az cosmosdb sql container create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --account-name myapp-cosmos \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --partition-key-path "/customerId" \
  --throughput 400  # start with 400 RU/s

# Switch to autoscale: min 10% of max, max 10,000 RU/s
az cosmosdb sql container throughput migrate \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --account-name myapp-cosmos \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --throughput-type autoscale

az cosmosdb sql container throughput update \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --account-name myapp-cosmos \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --max-throughput 10000
  # Cosmos now scales 1,000–10,000 RU/s automatically; billed per hour at actual peak
: Uptime guarantee expressed as SLA %. Availability Zones provide 99.99% SLA by isolating datacenter failures. Composite SLA = product of all dependencies.
: Designed capacity to handle growth. Vertical (bigger resource) or horizontal (more instances). Horizontal scaling requires stateless application design.
: Automatic scaling in response to actual demand. Azure Monitor Autoscale, KEDA, and Azure Functions Consumption plan are primary mechanisms. Converts peak-capacity CAPEX into pay-per-use OPEX.
Exam distinction: availability = is it up; scalability = can it grow; elasticity = does it grow automatically.
